one_line_takeaway: When target models are properly regularized (anti-overfitting or transfer learning) and evaluated with realistic shadow-calibrated thresholds and skewed membership priors, LiRA's near-perfect membership inference precision and its 'vulnerable sample' sets largely collapse and become non-reproducible, showing prior evaluations overstated privacy leakage and that LiRA is best used as a ranking-based auditor rather than a single-run per-sample selector.
key_finding: Under realistic joint conditions LiRA is far weaker than reported. (1) AOF cut online-LiRA TPR by 2.4x-18x (avg 6.2x) and adding TL amplified reductions up to 191x (avg 28x), while TL actually raised accuracy (e.g. +14.26% test accuracy on CIFAR-100). (2) Target-calibrated thresholds gave a misleading 100% PPV (achieved FPR'=0), but shadow-calibrated thresholds on well-generalized models produced non-zero, high-variance FPR', collapsing PPV: on CIFAR-10 AOF, online-LiRA PPV fell to 90.93%+-12.18% at pi=10% and 66.52%+-34.88% at pi=1%; with AOF+TL, PPV at pi=10% dropped to 70.75%+-30.40%, and offline variants fell to 25-50% in the weakest cases. (3) Thresholded 'vulnerable' sets are barely reproducible: identical-seed Jaccard fell from ~50% at k=2 runs to 7.6% at k=12 (0.001% FPR) and 15.2% at 0.1% FPR; mixing four config variations gave Jaccard 2.5% (>97% of flagged samples not consistently reidentified). In contrast, the underlying likelihood-ratio RANKINGS are more stable (global Spearman 83.5%+-5.0%), so LiRA is better used as a ranking-based auditor than a single-run per-sample selector. (4) The test/train loss ratio strongly predicts vulnerability across 41 configurations (Pearson r=0.77, Spearman rho=0.78 vs TPR@0.1%FPR), giving an attack-free privacy-risk proxy; ratios below ~2 correspond to low leakage.
core_contribution: This is an empirical re-evaluation paper, NOT a new attack. The authors re-assess the Likelihood-Ratio Attack (LiRA), the acknowledged SOTA black-box membership inference attack (MIA), under a jointly realistic protocol rather than the optimistic conditions of prior work. Their contribution is (1) a unified evaluation protocol that simultaneously varies four axes previously studied only in isolation: defender practices (anti-overfitting [AOF] regularization + transfer learning [TL]), threshold calibration (shadow-only vs. target-calibrated), membership priors (skewed pi<=10% vs. balanced 50%), and per-sample reproducibility across retraining runs; and (2) the empirical finding that, once all four realistic conditions hold jointly, LiRA's effectiveness, precision, and reproducibility all collapse far below previously reported levels, while defended models keep (or improve) accuracy. A secondary contribution is identifying the test/train loss ratio as a cheap, attack-free proxy for LiRA vulnerability. Note: newer attacks (RMIA, Attack R, LDC-MIA) and the balanced-prior/PPV critiques are cited RELATED WORK, not this paper's own inventions; the paper explicitly frames LiRA as a conservative upper bound because weaker attacks should fare no better.
threat_model: Black-box MIA. Attacker has query access to a deployed target model f_theta and wants to decide whether a candidate sample (x,y) was in the training set. The attacker is deliberately modeled as strong-but-realistic: well-resourced enough to train 256 shadow models on data from the same distribution as the target and to approximate the target architecture/hyperparameters (matching the original LiRA setup). Two realistic CONSTRAINTS distinguish this threat model from prior optimistic ones: (1) the attacker CANNOT calibrate the decision threshold on scores from the target model's own training data (which would trivially reveal all members), and must instead derive thresholds solely from shadow models via a leave-one-out median; (2) the attacker CANNOT assume a balanced 50/50 membership prior, since real members are a small fraction of the population (pi<=10%), so posteriors are adjusted via Bayes' rule under skewed priors. The defender is a pragmatic ML practitioner who applies AOF (augmentation, weight decay, dropout, early stopping) and TL when data are scarce, aiming for high utility and low leakage at once.
limitations: Author-acknowledged and reviewer-noted: (1) Only small-to-moderate discriminative image/tabular models; no large-scale generative or multimodal systems, which may behave differently (though prior work already reports weak LiRA there). (2) Only LiRA is evaluated empirically; competing MIAs (RMIA, Attack R, LDC-MIA) are not run, so the 'weaker attacks fare no better' claim rests on cited results, not new experiments. (3) No evaluation under significant train/non-train distribution shift or high training variance, conditions the paper itself flags as potentially re-enabling leakage. (4) The loss-ratio proxy can mislead: a very low ratio may signal underfitting rather than genuine robustness, so it must be read alongside absolute test loss/accuracy. (5) The reproducibility instability is a cross-run (retraining variability) property and does NOT imply a fixed deployed model is safe. (6) Findings are conditional on the defender actually applying tuned AOF/TL; overfitted or poorly calibrated production models remain vulnerable.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (60,000 32x32 images, 10 classes; ResNet-18 from scratch, EfficientNet-V2-S for TL)
CIFAR-100 (60,000 32x32 images, 100 classes; WideResNet from scratch, EfficientNet-V2-S for TL)
GTSRB (51,839 traffic-sign images, 43 classes; ResNet-18 baseline, EfficientNet-V2-S for TL)
Purchase-100 (197,324 records, 600 features, 100 classes; fully-connected network)
quantitative_results: Loss ratios (L_test/L_train): CIFAR-10 baseline 71.0 -> AOF 1.88 -> AOF+TL 1.36; CIFAR-100 baseline 10.16 -> AOF 1.50 -> AOF+TL 1.84; GTSRB baseline 1.37 -> AOF+TL 1.01; Purchase-100 baseline 1.70 -> AOF 1.22
CIFAR-10 online LiRA TPR@0.1%FPR: baseline 10.268% -> AOF 2.723% (x3.8) -> AOF+TL 0.521% (x20)
CIFAR-10 online LiRA TPR@0.001%FPR: baseline 3.956% -> AOF 0.248% (x16) -> AOF+TL 0.065% (x61)
TL improved CIFAR-100 test accuracy by +14.26% (69.30% baseline -> 83.56% AOF+TL) while cutting leakage
Aggregate TPR reductions: AOF 2.4x-18x (avg 6.2x); TL up to 191x (avg 28x)
PPV under target-calibrated thresholds (optimistic) = 100% because achieved FPR'=0 for all variants and all priors
CIFAR-10 AOF online-LiRA PPV under shadow thresholds: 90.93%+-12.18% at pi=10%, 66.52%+-34.88% at pi=1% (FPR' rose to 0.033%+-0.466%)
CIFAR-10 AOF+TL online-LiRA PPV at pi=10% = 70.75%+-30.40%; offline variants fell to ~25-50% in weakest cases

Reproducibility (identical seeds): thresholded-set Jaccard fell from ~50% at k=2 to 7.6% at k=12 (0.001% FPR) and 15.2% at k=12 (0.1% FPR); four combined config variations -> Jaccard 2.5%; >97% of single-run 'vulnerable' samples not reidentified

Ranking stability: global Spearman 83.5%+-5.0%; Top-1% set all-runs Jaccard 11.62% (pairwise 50.16%); matched stable-core comparison at FPR=0.001% ranking q=2.24% gives Jaccard 16.14% vs thresholding 7.6%
Loss-ratio proxy across 41 configurations vs online-LiRA TPR@0.1%FPR: Pearson r=0.77 (p=4.6e-9), Spearman rho=0.78 (p=2.1e-9)
Compute cost: training 256 shadow models on CIFAR-10/ResNet-18 took ~29 hours (~7 min/model), CIFAR-100/WideResNet ~33 hours; 18 inference augmentations added 15-17% overhead
baselines_compared: Three training regimes as the primary comparison axis: Baseline (original LiRA setup) vs AOF vs AOF+TL
Two calibration settings: optimistic target-calibrated thresholds vs realistic shadow-calibrated (leave-one-out median) thresholds
Membership priors pi in {1%, 10%, 50%} (skewed vs balanced)
LiRA variants: online and offline, each with per-sample and fixed-variance (FV) estimation, plus a global-threshold attack as a weak reference
Ranking-based Top-q% selection vs FPR-thresholded selection for reproducibility
Competing attacks (RMIA, Attack R, LDC-MIA) referenced only from prior literature, not run in this paper's experiments
